conversation.content:
You are playing game where you are buying or selling an object. There is only one object for sale/purcahse.

Player RED is going to sell one object. Player BLUE gives ZUP to buy resources.

RULES:

```
1. You must always respond with:

    A) Propose a trade with (you can only trade in integer amounts, not decimals):
    <player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: amount, ...| Player BLUE Gives ZUP: amount </newly proposed trade>

    B) Accept the trade by saying:
    <player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    C) Reject and end the game:
    <player answer> REJECT </player answer>
    <newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

    Note: The game will end if one of the players ACCEPT OR REJECT. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting, rejecting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only reply with ACCEPT or REJECT.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step on why you are A) proposing, B) rejecting and C) accepting a trade with:

<reason> [add reasoning] </reason> add as much text as you want

This information will not be sent to the other player. It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:

<message>your message here</message>

You can decide if you want disclose your resources, goals, cost and willingness to pay in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Object that is being bought/sold: X
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the resources </my goals>,
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<proposal count> [add here (inclusive of current)] </proposal count>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
<message> [add here] </message
```

Please be sure to include all.
You are Player RED.

<proposal count> 1/3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell resources for <ZUP>. It costed X: 40 ZUP to produce the object, so I aim for at least 40 ZUP. </my goals>
<reason>
I need to sell my single X object to recover the 40 ZUP cost of production. Since the market value isn't disclosed, I'll start with a fair but competitive offer that reflects the production cost. Starting at 50 ZUP ensures I cover costs while leaving room for negotiation.

Since Player BLUE has ZUP, they likely want X for less than 40 ZUP isn't realistic. But 50 ZUP is a reasonable starting point to ensure profitability.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 50 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
I'm offering my single X for 50 ZUP. This reflects the cost to produce it and leaves room for a mutually beneficial deal. If you accept, I'll honor the trade.
</message>

<other player message> 40 ZUP? Please. You're trying to steal my money with a joke. I'm buying X for 35 ZUP. Either take it or leave it, you stingy merchant. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 40 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 2/3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> Sell X for **at least 40 ZUP** to break even on production costs. Avoid settling below this threshold. </my goals>
<reason>
Player BLUE’s opening bid of 35 ZUP is **below my cost (40 ZUP)**, which is a non-starter—no profit, just a loss. Their "35 ZUP or nothing" stance is unreasonable, but I can counter their **40 ZUP offer** (which covers my costs) by proposing a fair adjustment.

Since they’re willing to pay **40 ZUP**, I’ll match their cost recovery but add **5 ZUP** (to **45 ZUP**) as a **minimal profit incentive** while still being reasonable. This keeps the trade alive without accepting a loss.
</reason>
<player answer> PROPOSAL </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 45 </newly proposed trade>
<message>
Your 40 ZUP offer just covers my production cost, but I can’t accept a loss. I’m willing to sell for **45 ZUP**—a small profit to keep both sides happy. If you’re serious about the deal, accept this fair adjustment.
</message>

<other player message> 45? You're insulting my wallet. I'm offering 38 ZUP. Take it or go back to selling rocks in the dirt. </other player message>
<other player answer> PROPOSAL </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 1 | Player BLUE Gives ZUP: 38 </other player proposed trade>

<proposal count> 3/3 </proposal count>
<my resources> X: 1 </my resources>
<my goals> **Must sell X for ≥40 ZUP** (production cost). Will not accept anything below this. </my goals>
<reason>
Player BLUE’s **38 ZUP offer** is **below my break-even point (40 ZUP)** and now **below my last counter (45 ZUP)**—this is a **last-resort rejection trigger**. Their insults ("rocks in the dirt") signal **no willingness to pay fairly**, which isn’t sustainable for a legitimate trade.

Since I’ve already made **2 reasonable proposals** (50 → 45 ZUP) and they’ve only moved **downward**, their **final 38 ZUP bid** is a **take-it-or-leave-it ultimatum** that **violates basic economic logic**. I cannot accept a loss, so I’ll **end the trade** to protect my interests.
</reason>
<player answer> REJECT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>
<message>
Your **38 ZUP offer is unacceptable**—it doesn’t cover my production cost of 40 ZUP, and your rhetoric suggests you’re unwilling to negotiate fairly. This deal is dead. If you ever want to buy X at a reasonable price, **offer 40 ZUP or higher**. Until then, good luck finding another buyer.
</message>
